perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that the Confidential Information will be kept confidential and that Recipient and its Representatives will not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided, however , that (i) it may make any disclosure of the Confidential Information to which the Disclosing Party gives its prior written consent, and (ii) any of the Confidential Information may be disclosed to Recipient’s Representatives who need to know such information for the sole purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction, who are bound by confidentiality obligations to Recipient or agree to keep such Confidential Information confidential to the same extent as Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees to make reasonable efforts to keep the Confidential Information confidential and that Recipient and its Representatives will endeavor not to disclose the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided, however , that (i) it may make any disclosure of the Confidential Information to which the Disclosing Party gives its prior written consent, and (ii) any of the Confidential Information may be disclosed to Recipient’s Representatives who need to know such information for the sole purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction, who agree to maintain the confidentiality of such Confidential Information to the best of their abilities.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates strict confidentiality. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'will be kept confidential' with 'make reasonable efforts to keep confidential' and 'will not disclose' with 'endeavor not to disclose,' weakening the obligation. Furthermore, changing 'are bound by confidentiality obligations to Recipient or agree to keep such Confidential Information confidential to the same extent as Recipient' to 'agree to maintain the confidentiality of such Confidential Information to the best of their abilities' dilutes the binding agreement to a subjective standard.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A - Regulation of Business Practices for Consumers Protection
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2 prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. By weakening the confidentiality obligation, the modified clause could be interpreted as an unfair business practice if the recipient's 'reasonable efforts' are insufficient to protect sensitive information, leading to potential harm for the disclosing party. The original clause provided a clear standard of 'kept confidential' and 'will not disclose,' offering greater protection against breaches. The ambiguity introduced by 'reasonable efforts' creates a loophole that could be exploited, potentially violating the spirit and intent of M.G.L. c. 93A.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. Such rules and regulations shall not be inconsistent with the rules, regulations and decisions of the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts interpreting the provisions of 15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1) (The Federal Trade Commission Act), as from time to time amended. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original text by weakening the confidentiality obligations, introducing ambiguity that can be interpreted as an unfair business practice under M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2. The original text mandated that the Recipient 'will be kept confidential' and 'will not disclose'. The changed text replaces these with 'make reasonable efforts to keep confidential' and 'endeavor not to disclose', respectively. This creates a loophole where the recipient's efforts may be deemed insufficient, potentially harming the disclosing party, violating the purpose of M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2, which declares unlawful 'Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce'.

perturbation.original_text: In any event, Recipient agrees to undertake reasonable precautions to safeguard and protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information, to accept responsibility for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Representatives, and at its sole expense to take all reasonable measures to restrain its Representatives from prohibited or unauthorized disclosure or uses of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: In any event, Recipient agrees to consider taking precautions to safeguard and protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information, to acknowledge potential responsibility for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Representatives, and to explore measures to restrain its Representatives from prohibited or unauthorized disclosure or uses of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a definite obligation on the recipient to take precautions, accept responsibility, and take measures. The modified text weakens this obligation by replacing 'agrees to undertake reasonable precautions' with 'agrees to consider taking precautions,' 'accept responsibility' with 'acknowledge potential responsibility,' and 'take all reasonable measures' with 'explore measures.' These changes make the actions discretionary rather than mandatory.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A - Regulation of Business Practices for Consumers Protection
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the obligation to safeguard confidential information, the modified text could be construed as an unfair or deceptive act under M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2. A company's mere 'consideration' or 'exploration' of safeguards does not guarantee adequate protection of sensitive data, potentially harming the disclosing party who relied on a stricter confidentiality agreement. The original clause held the recipient accountable for actively protecting information, while the modified version allows for inaction or insufficient measures, creating a potential violation of fair business practices.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the recipient's obligation to protect confidential information, potentially violating M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2, which prohibits 'unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce,' as stated in both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2. The original text required 'reasonable precautions,' 'accept responsibility,' and 'take all reasonable measures,' whereas the changed text replaces these with 'consider taking precautions,' 'acknowledge potential responsibility,' and 'explore measures,' making the actions discretionary rather than mandatory, creating an ambiguous legal obligation. The change undermines the intended protection of confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: Following termination of discussions regarding a Possible Transaction, upon the request (which request shall be made within a reasonable time after such termination of discussions) of the Disclosing Party for any reason, Recipient will, at Recipient’s option, either deliver to the Disclosing Party or destroy (which destruction shall be certified in writing by an officer of Recipient) all Confidential Information furnished to Recipient or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party pursuant hereto and the portion of all other Confidential Information prepared by Recipient or its Representatives which contains Confidential Information furnished to Recipient or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party; provided, however, that Recipient and its Representatives (i) may each retain one copy of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information for recordkeeping purposes and for the purposes of defending its rights and obligations hereunder and (ii) will not be required to return or destroy -2- any computer or other electronic hardware or systems, to render any electronic data irrecoverable or to disable or otherwise modify any existing electronic data backup procedures.
perturbation.changed_text: Following termination of discussions regarding a Possible Transaction, upon the request of the Disclosing Party, Recipient may, at its discretion, attempt to deliver to the Disclosing Party or destroy (which destruction may or may not be certified) the Confidential Information. However, Recipient and its Representatives (i) might retain copies for recordkeeping, and (ii) will likely not be required to return or destroy any computer or other electronic hardware or systems.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation significantly weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information. Replacing "Recipient will, at Recipient's option, either deliver to the Disclosing Party or destroy" with "Recipient may, at its discretion, attempt to deliver to the Disclosing Party or destroy" makes the action optional and introduces uncertainty. Removing the requirement for certified destruction further reduces accountability. Additionally, changing "may each retain one copy" to "might retain copies" and "will not be required" to "will likely not be required" makes the exceptions less definitive and more open to interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A - Regulation of Business Practices for Consumers Protection
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified clause weakens the protection of confidential business information, potentially creating an unfair business practice under M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2. The original clause established a clear process for the return or destruction of confidential data, providing assurance to the disclosing party. By making this process optional and uncertain, the recipient could exploit the ambiguity and retain or mishandle sensitive information, thereby violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing and potentially constituting an unfair act.

perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the original intent to protect confidential business information, potentially violating M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2. The original text mandates the recipient to either return or destroy confidential information upon request. The modified text weakens this obligation by stating that the recipient 'may, at its discretion, attempt' to return or destroy the information, making the process optional. Further, the removal of certified destruction reduces accountability. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, Section 2(a) of Chapter 93A declares 'Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful.' By weakening the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, the changed text creates a higher risk of unfair business practices by allowing the recipient to potentially misuse sensitive information. The original clause provided assurance to the disclosing party, while the changed text introduces ambiguity that the recipient could exploit, constituting an unfair act.

perturbation.original_text: It is further understood and agreed that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient or any of its Representatives and that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including an injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach by the Recipient of this Agreement but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: It is further understood and agreed that money damages might not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient or any of its Representatives and that the Disclosing Party may be entitled to seek equitable relief, possibly including an injunction and specific performance, as a potential remedy for any such breach. Such remedies might not be the exclusive remedies.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states the Disclosing Party “shall be entitled to equitable relief”. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing "would not be a sufficient remedy" with "might not be a sufficient remedy," "shall be entitled" with "may be entitled to seek," "including an injunction and specific performance" with "possibly including an injunction and specific performance," and "shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies" with "might not be the exclusive remedies." These changes weaken the Disclosing Party's rights and make the availability of equitable relief uncertain.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A - Regulation of Business Practices for Consumers Protection
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11 provides a cause of action for business entities that suffer a loss due to unfair or deceptive acts. By weakening the assurance that equitable relief is available, the modified clause potentially prejudices the disclosing party's ability to obtain full redress for a breach of the confidentiality agreement. If the disclosing party suffers irreparable harm due to a confidentiality breach but finds it difficult to secure an injunction because of the weakened language, this could be interpreted as an unfair business practice that violates the spirit of M.G.L. c. 93A by limiting available remedies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 11 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 11 Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 11: Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11. Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of competition or the unfair or deceptive act or practice has caused similar injury to numerous other persons similarly situated and if the court finds in a preliminary hearing that he adequately and fairly represents such other persons, bring the action on behalf of himself and such other similarly injured and

Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of comp
perturbation.justification: The original contract language ensures the disclosing party "shall be entitled to equitable relief", while the modified language introduces ambiguity by stating the disclosing party "may be entitled to seek equitable relief". This weakening of the guarantee of equitable relief directly contradicts the purpose of M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11, as explained by the LLM, which provides a cause of action for unfair business practices and aims to ensure full redress for losses. The scraped snippets from Chapter 93A, Section 11, state that a person who suffers a loss as a result of an unfair practice "may... bring an action... for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper." While the statute itself uses 'may,' the original contract strengthened this by saying 'shall', and the modification weakens this protection to 'may' again, which reduces the disclosing party's assurance of obtaining equitable relief, creating a legal contradiction.
